templates.title: Recover Unpaid Wages or Salary
templates.title_hi: बकाया वेतन या मजदूरी की वसूली
templates.description: Steps to claim wages, salary, or overtime that an employer has withheld or delayed, whether you are a factory worker, a shop employee, or a private-sector staff member.

documents.name: Proof of employment
documents.why: Establishes that you worked for the employer and were entitled to wages.
documents.how_to_get: Keep your appointment or offer letter, ID card, contract, or attendance record. If you have none, WhatsApp chats or emails from a supervisor also help.

documents.name: Wage and payment records
documents.why: Shows the agreed pay and how much is actually unpaid.
documents.how_to_get: Collect payslips, salary-account bank statements, and records of any partial payments made to you.

documents.name: Written record of amount owed
documents.why: A dated chronology helps the authority calculate the exact dues quickly.
documents.how_to_get: Prepare a simple table: pay period, amount due, amount received, balance.

documents.name: Communication about pay
documents.why: Messages promising or refusing payment are strong evidence.
documents.how_to_get: Save emails, SMS, and WhatsApp messages about salary, deductions, or termination.

steps: Send a written demand (email or registered letter) to your employer asking for the exact unpaid amount and keep proof of sending.
If ignored, file a claim with the Inspector/Authority under the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, or approach the Labour Commissioner for your area.
For workmen, you may raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Court under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947.
Attend hearings with your documents; the authority can order payment plus compensation.
If you cannot afford a lawyer, seek free legal aid from the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA).

deadlines.what: Claim before the Authority under the Payment of Wages Act, 1936
deadlines.timeframe: Within 12 months of the date the wages became payable (delay may be condoned for sufficient cause)

deadlines.what: Ordinary civil suit for recovery of money
deadlines.timeframe: Within 3 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

tips: Never hand over your original documents; share photocopies only.
Note down the names and designations of witnesses who saw you working.
Deductions from wages are legal only in the limited cases listed in the Payment of Wages Act.
templates.helpline: Shram Suvidha / Ministry of Labour grievance helpline; National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) 15100

templates.title: Get Back a Rental Security Deposit
templates.title_hi: किराए की जमानत राशि वापस पाना
templates.description: How to recover a security or advance deposit that a landlord refuses to return, or from which unfair deductions have been made, after you vacate a rented home.

documents.name: Rent agreement / lease deed
documents.why: Proves the deposit amount and the terms for its refund.
documents.how_to_get: Use your signed copy of the registered or notarised agreement.

documents.name: Deposit payment proof
documents.why: Shows how much you paid and when.
documents.how_to_get: Keep the receipt, bank transfer entry, UPI record, or written acknowledgement from the landlord.

documents.name: Move-in and move-out condition evidence
documents.why: Counters false claims of damage used to withhold the deposit.
documents.how_to_get: Dated photos or videos of the property when you entered and when you left, plus any inventory list.

documents.name: Handover proof
documents.why: Shows you returned possession and cleared dues.
documents.how_to_get: Keep proof of key handover, final electricity/water meter readings, and last rent payment.

steps: Send a polite written refund request to the landlord stating the amount and a reasonable deadline.
If refused, send a formal legal notice (a lawyer or legal-aid clinic can help draft it).
If still unpaid, file a civil suit for recovery, or approach the Rent Controller / Rent Tribunal if your state's Rent Act covers your tenancy.
Small amounts can sometimes be pursued through a consumer forum or the state's model-tenancy authority where notified.

deadlines.what: Civil suit to recover the deposit (money due)
deadlines.timeframe: Within 3 years from the date the refund became due, under the Limitation Act, 1963

deadlines.what: Notice/refund period under a Rent Control or Model Tenancy Act
deadlines.timeframe: Varies by state; many Model Tenancy laws require refund at handover or within a fixed period stated in that state's Act

tips: Do a joint move-out inspection with the landlord if possible and get the condition noted in writing.
Reasonable deductions for actual damage or unpaid dues are allowed; blanket forfeiture usually is not.
Keep all communication in writing so verbal promises can be proven.
templates.helpline: State Rent Authority (where notified); National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) 15100

templates.title: Protection from Domestic Violence
templates.title_hi: घरेलू हिंसा से सुरक्षा
templates.description: How a woman facing physical, emotional, sexual, verbal, or economic abuse at home can seek protection, residence, and maintenance orders under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005.

documents.name: Identity and relationship proof
documents.why: Shows you share or shared a household with the respondent.
documents.how_to_get: Aadhaar, marriage certificate, ration card, or any document linking you to the shared home.

documents.name: Evidence of abuse
documents.why: Supports your application for protection and relief.
documents.how_to_get: Medical reports of injuries, photographs, threatening messages, call recordings, and names of witnesses.

documents.name: Record of the incidents
documents.why: A dated account helps the Protection Officer and court understand the pattern.
documents.how_to_get: Write down each incident with date, place, and what happened.

documents.name: Financial documents
documents.why: Needed to claim maintenance and residence rights.
documents.how_to_get: Bank statements, proof of household expenses, and any record of the respondent's income.

steps: Contact a Protection Officer, a registered service provider, or the police to file a Domestic Incident Report.
File an application before the Magistrate seeking protection, residence, maintenance, custody, or compensation orders.
Request an urgent interim/ex-parte order if you are in immediate danger.
You can also file a criminal complaint for cruelty (BNS Section 85/86, earlier IPC 498A) if applicable.
Seek free legal aid and shelter-home support if you have no safe place to stay.

deadlines.what: Application under the Domestic Violence Act, 2005
deadlines.timeframe: No fixed limitation period; abuse can be reported at any time, but acting promptly strengthens the case (courts generally expect reasonably recent grievances)

tips: You have a legal right to stay in the shared household; you cannot simply be thrown out.
Preserve evidence safely, ideally with a trusted person outside the home.
Call 181 or 112 immediately if you are in danger right now.

templates.title: Complain About a Defective Product or Bad Service
templates.title_hi: खराब उत्पाद या सेवा की शिकायत
templates.description: How to seek a refund, replacement, or compensation for a defective product, deficient service, or unfair trade practice under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019.

documents.name: Proof of purchase
documents.why: Establishes the transaction and its value, which also decides the forum.
documents.how_to_get: Keep the invoice, bill, order confirmation, or payment record.

documents.name: Warranty / guarantee card
documents.why: Shows the promised terms the seller or manufacturer must honour.
documents.how_to_get: Use the card or the terms printed on the packaging or website.

documents.name: Evidence of the defect
documents.why: Demonstrates the product or service failed.
documents.how_to_get: Photos, videos, service-centre reports, or an expert opinion where needed.

documents.name: Complaint correspondence
documents.why: Shows you gave the seller a chance to fix the issue.
documents.how_to_get: Save emails, chat transcripts, and complaint-ticket numbers with the company.

steps: Raise a complaint directly with the seller or manufacturer and keep the ticket number.
If unresolved, file a complaint with the National Consumer Helpline (1915) or online at the e-Daakhil portal.
File a formal complaint before the District, State, or National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission based on the claim amount.
Attend hearings and produce your documents; the Commission can order refund, replacement, and compensation.

deadlines.what: Filing a consumer complaint
deadlines.timeframe: Within 2 years from the date the cause of action arose (delay may be condoned if you show sufficient cause), under Section 69 of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019

tips: You can file the complaint yourself; a lawyer is not compulsory.
Filing is free or low-cost for smaller claims.
The complaint can be filed where you live, not only where the seller is located.

templates.title: Report Online / UPI Financial Fraud
templates.title_hi: ऑनलाइन / यूपीआई धोखाधड़ी की रिपोर्ट
templates.description: Immediate steps to report and try to recover money lost to UPI, net-banking, card, or online-payment fraud, and to limit your liability.

documents.name: Transaction details
documents.why: Banks and the cyber cell need exact details to freeze and trace the money.
documents.how_to_get: Note the amount, date, time, UPI transaction ID / UTR number, and the receiver's UPI ID or account.

documents.name: Bank statement / SMS alerts
documents.why: Proves the unauthorised debit.
documents.how_to_get: Take screenshots of transaction SMS/app notifications and download the statement.

documents.name: Fraud communication
documents.why: Shows how you were deceived (phishing link, fake call, fake app).
documents.how_to_get: Save the caller's number, screenshots of messages, links, and any screen-share app you were told to install.

steps: Immediately call the national cybercrime helpline 1930 and report on cybercrime.gov.in to trigger a freeze on the fraudulent transfer.
Report the unauthorised transaction to your bank in writing without delay and ask them to block the card/account.
Change your UPI PIN, net-banking, and email passwords.
File a written complaint at the nearest cyber police station or the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal.
Follow up with the bank's grievance officer and, if unresolved, the Banking Ombudsman.

deadlines.what: Reporting the unauthorised transaction to your bank for limited/zero liability
deadlines.timeframe: As early as possible; under RBI's customer-liability rules, reporting within 3 working days of the transaction generally means zero customer liability, and delay increases your liability

deadlines.what: Reporting to cyber helpline for a chance of freezing funds
deadlines.timeframe: Within the 'golden hour' - the sooner the better, ideally within the first few hours

tips: Never share OTP, PIN, or CVV; no genuine bank asks for them.
Do not install any screen-sharing or remote-access app on a stranger's instruction.
Report even small amounts; it helps trace organised fraud networks.
templates.helpline: Cyber Crime Helpline 1930; report at cybercrime.gov.in

templates.title: Police Refusing to Register Your FIR
templates.title_hi: पुलिस द्वारा एफआईआर दर्ज करने से इनकार
templates.description: What to do when a police station refuses to register a First Information Report for a cognizable offence.

documents.name: Written complaint
documents.why: You are entitled to submit your complaint in writing and get an acknowledgement.
documents.how_to_get: Write a clear, dated account of the offence and keep a copy for yourself.

documents.name: Proof of the incident
documents.why: Supports registration and later investigation.
documents.how_to_get: Collect medical reports, photos, CCTV, witness names, or any relevant evidence.

documents.name: Proof of attempts to report
documents.why: Shows the police declined to act.
documents.how_to_get: Keep acknowledgement receipts, the postal proof if sent by registered post, or a diary note of the date and officer.

steps: Submit your written complaint at the police station and insist on a receiving stamp or diary entry.
If refused, send the written complaint to the Superintendent of Police (SP) by registered post under BNSS Section 173(4) (earlier CrPC 154(3)).
If still no action, file a complaint before the jurisdictional Magistrate under BNSS Section 175(3) (earlier CrPC 156(3)) directing the police to register and investigate.
For serious offences you may also approach the State Human Rights Commission or the High Court.
Keep copies of every complaint and postal receipt.

deadlines.what: Registering an FIR for a cognizable offence
deadlines.timeframe: The police must register it without delay; there is generally no limitation bar on lodging an FIR itself, though prosecution of some minor offences has time limits under the law

tips: For certain serious offences against women, refusal to register an FIR is itself punishable.
Always keep a signed, dated copy of your complaint.
You can send the complaint online or by email to senior officers as additional proof.

templates.title: Facing Illegal Eviction by a Landlord
templates.title_hi: मकान मालिक द्वारा गैरकानूनी बेदखली
templates.description: How a tenant can respond when a landlord tries to evict them by force, by cutting off water or electricity, or without following due legal process.

documents.name: Rent agreement
documents.why: Proves your lawful tenancy and possession.
documents.how_to_get: Use your signed copy of the agreement; if none exists, rent receipts help.

documents.name: Rent payment proof
documents.why: Shows you are not in default and have a right to stay.
documents.how_to_get: Bank entries, UPI records, or written receipts for rent paid.

documents.name: Evidence of the illegal act
documents.why: Supports a police complaint or injunction.
documents.how_to_get: Photos/videos of locked doors, disconnected utilities, or damage, plus witness names and any threatening messages.

steps: Inform the landlord in writing that forced eviction without a court order is illegal.
If threatened or locked out, file a police complaint immediately; forced dispossession can be a criminal offence.
File a civil suit for injunction to restrain the landlord and to restore possession if you were dispossessed.
Where a state Rent Control Act applies, only the Rent Controller can order eviction on the grounds listed in that Act.
Seek free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer.

deadlines.what: Suit to recover possession after being dispossessed without consent (Specific Relief Act, Section 6)
deadlines.timeframe: Within 6 months of dispossession

deadlines.what: Ordinary suit based on title to immovable property
deadlines.timeframe: Within 12 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

tips: A landlord cannot legally evict you by force, changing locks, or cutting utilities; only a court can order eviction.
Keep paying rent (or deposit it in court) so you are not treated as a defaulter.
Record the date and time of any eviction attempt immediately.

templates.title: Sexual Harassment at the Workplace
templates.title_hi: कार्यस्थल पर यौन उत्पीड़न
templates.description: How a woman can complain about sexual harassment at work under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013 (POSH Act).

documents.name: Written complaint
documents.why: Starts the formal inquiry by the Internal Committee.
documents.how_to_get: Write a dated account of each incident with names, dates, and places.

documents.name: Evidence of harassment
documents.why: Supports your complaint before the committee.
documents.how_to_get: Save messages, emails, CCTV references, and names of colleagues who witnessed the conduct.

documents.name: Employment proof
documents.why: Shows you and the respondent are connected to the same workplace.
documents.how_to_get: ID card, appointment letter, or salary slip.

steps: Submit a written complaint to the Internal Committee (IC) of your organisation, or to the Local Committee (LC) if your workplace has fewer than 10 employees or the respondent is the employer.
The committee must conduct an inquiry and can recommend interim relief such as transfer or leave.
Cooperate with the inquiry and submit your evidence and witnesses.
You may also file a criminal complaint (for example under BNS provisions on outraging modesty / sexual harassment).
If dissatisfied with the committee's findings, you can appeal as provided under the Act.

deadlines.what: Filing a complaint before the Internal/Local Committee
deadlines.timeframe: Within 3 months of the incident, or of the last incident in a series; the committee may extend this by a further 3 months for recorded reasons

deadlines.what: Appeal against the committee's recommendation
deadlines.timeframe: Within 90 days of the recommendation

tips: Every workplace with 10 or more employees is legally required to have an Internal Committee.
Retaliation for filing a genuine complaint is prohibited under the Act.
Your identity and the proceedings are meant to be kept confidential.
templates.helpline: Women Helpline 181; SHe-Box online complaint portal

templates.title: Dowry Demand or Harassment
templates.title_hi: दहेज की मांग या उत्पीड़न
templates.description: How to act against demands for dowry and cruelty connected to dowry, under the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961 and the cruelty provisions of the BNS.

documents.name: Evidence of dowry demand
documents.why: Proves the illegal demand was made.
documents.how_to_get: Save messages, call recordings, emails, or witness statements about the demand.

documents.name: Record of items given
documents.why: Establishes what was given as dowry and by whom.
documents.how_to_get: Keep a list of cash, jewellery, and goods with any receipts, photos, or wedding records.

documents.name: Evidence of cruelty or harassment
documents.why: Supports a complaint of cruelty linked to dowry.
documents.how_to_get: Medical reports, photographs of injuries, threatening messages, and names of witnesses.

steps: Report the demand or harassment to the police; both giving/taking dowry and demanding it are offences.
File a complaint for cruelty under BNS Section 85/86 (earlier IPC 498A) if you face cruelty from a husband or his relatives.
You may also seek protection and maintenance under the Domestic Violence Act, 2005.
Preserve all evidence and note the names of relatives involved.
Seek free legal aid and, if needed, protection or shelter support.

deadlines.what: Complaint under the Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961
deadlines.timeframe: Act promptly; some offences under this Act carry statutory time limits for taking cognizance, so early complaint is important

deadlines.what: Cruelty complaint (BNS Section 85/86)
deadlines.timeframe: No fixed limitation for this serious offence, but delay should be explained

tips: Demanding, giving, or taking dowry are all offences; the woman and her family are protected complainants.
Keep evidence with a trusted person outside the marital home.
Call 181 or 112 if you are in immediate danger.

templates.title: Cheque Bounce (Dishonour of Cheque)
templates.title_hi: चेक बाउंस (चेक का अनादर)
templates.description: How to recover money when a cheque given to you bounces, under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881.

documents.name: The dishonoured cheque
documents.why: Central evidence of the debt and the dishonour.
documents.how_to_get: Keep the original bounced cheque safely.

documents.name: Bank return memo
documents.why: Shows the cheque was returned unpaid and the reason.
documents.how_to_get: Your bank issues a cheque-return memo when the cheque bounces; collect it.

documents.name: Proof of the underlying debt
documents.why: Establishes the cheque was for a legally enforceable liability.
documents.how_to_get: Loan agreement, invoice, ledger, or messages showing the amount owed.

documents.name: Copy of the demand notice
documents.why: Sending the statutory notice is a mandatory step before filing.
documents.how_to_get: Keep a copy of the notice and the postal/courier receipt and acknowledgement.

steps: On dishonour, get the cheque-return memo from your bank.
Send a written demand notice to the drawer demanding payment within 15 days.
If the drawer does not pay within 15 days, file a criminal complaint under Section 138 before the Magistrate.
Attend hearings with your documents; you may also file a civil recovery suit in parallel.
Consider a mutual settlement, which courts encourage in cheque cases.

deadlines.what: Sending the demand notice to the drawer
deadlines.timeframe: Within 30 days of receiving the bank's cheque-return memo

deadlines.what: Drawer's window to pay after receiving the notice
deadlines.timeframe: 15 days from receipt of the notice

deadlines.what: Filing the complaint under Section 138
deadlines.timeframe: Within 1 month after the 15-day payment period expires (courts may condone delay for sufficient cause)

tips: The demand notice must be sent within 30 days of the return memo, or the case can fail; do not delay.
Send the notice by registered post and keep proof of dispatch.
You can present the cheque again within its validity, but the deadlines run from the dishonour you rely on.

templates.title: Property or Land Ownership Dispute
templates.title_hi: संपत्ति या भूमि स्वामित्व विवाद
templates.description: How to protect and assert your rights in a dispute over ownership, possession, boundaries, or encroachment of immovable property.

documents.name: Title documents
documents.why: Establish your ownership or interest in the property.
documents.how_to_get: Sale deed, gift deed, will, partition deed, or registered agreement; obtain certified copies from the Sub-Registrar if needed.

documents.name: Revenue and tax records
documents.why: Support possession and ownership claims.
documents.how_to_get: Mutation records, khata/patta, land-record extracts (RTC/7-12/jamabandi), and property-tax receipts from the local office or online portal.

documents.name: Survey / map records
documents.why: Fix boundaries in encroachment disputes.
documents.how_to_get: Apply for an official survey or obtain the survey sketch from the revenue department.

documents.name: Evidence of possession
documents.why: Possession is a key factor in property suits.
documents.how_to_get: Utility bills, photographs, and witness statements showing who occupies the property.

steps: Get certified copies of all title and revenue records and verify the chain of ownership.
Send a legal notice to the opposing party stating your claim.
File the appropriate civil suit - for declaration of title, possession, injunction, or partition.
Seek an interim injunction to stop any sale, construction, or dispossession during the case.
Consider mediation or Lok Adalat, which can settle property disputes faster.

deadlines.what: Suit for possession based on title to immovable property
deadlines.timeframe: Within 12 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

deadlines.what: Suit for possession after dispossession without consent (Specific Relief Act, Section 6)
deadlines.timeframe: Within 6 months of dispossession

deadlines.what: Suit to cancel a document or declare it void
deadlines.timeframe: Generally within 3 years from when the facts became known, under the Limitation Act, 1963

tips: Do not delay; adverse possession by another party can arise over long periods of uninterrupted possession.
Always work from certified copies and keep originals in a safe place.
Get any boundary settlement or family partition recorded in writing and registered.

templates.title: Compensation for a Road / Motor Accident
templates.title_hi: सड़क / मोटर दुर्घटना के लिए मुआवजा
templates.description: How an accident victim or the family of a deceased can claim compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT).

documents.name: FIR / police records
documents.why: Establishes how the accident happened and who was involved.
documents.how_to_get: Get a copy of the FIR, the charge sheet, and the accident report from the police station.

documents.name: Medical records
documents.why: Proves the injuries and treatment costs.
documents.how_to_get: Hospital discharge summary, bills, prescriptions, disability certificate, and (in fatal cases) the post-mortem report.

documents.name: Vehicle and insurance details
documents.why: Identify the insurer who pays the compensation.
documents.how_to_get: Registration number, insurance policy, and driving-licence details of the offending vehicle (police can help obtain these).

documents.name: Proof of income and dependency
documents.why: Determines the amount of compensation.
documents.how_to_get: Salary slips, income-tax returns, or income certificate; in death cases, proof of legal heirs and dependency.

steps: Ensure the accident is reported to the police and an FIR / accident report is recorded.
Get all medical treatment documented with bills and certificates.
File a claim petition before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) for the area where the accident occurred or where you reside.
The insurer of the offending vehicle is usually made a party and pays the award.
You can also access no-fault/interim relief schemes and, for hit-and-run cases, the government compensation scheme.

deadlines.what: Filing the claim petition before MACT
deadlines.timeframe: Within 6 months of the accident, as introduced by Section 166(3) of the Motor Vehicles Act (amended by the 2019 Act); file as early as possible and seek condonation if delayed

tips: Note the offending vehicle's number and photograph the scene if you safely can.
Keep every medical bill and prescription; small receipts add up in the claim.
Cashless treatment and interim relief may be available soon after the accident.

templates.title: Medical Negligence Complaint
templates.title_hi: चिकित्सा लापरवाही की शिकायत
templates.description: How to seek compensation or action when injury or death results from a doctor's or hospital's negligent treatment.

documents.name: Complete medical records
documents.why: Central evidence of the treatment given and where it went wrong.
documents.how_to_get: Request the full case file, prescriptions, test reports, operation notes, and discharge summary; hospitals must provide records on request.

documents.name: Payment receipts
documents.why: Shows a service was paid for, letting you approach a consumer forum.
documents.how_to_get: Keep all hospital and pharmacy bills and payment proofs.

documents.name: Second / expert medical opinion
documents.why: Helps establish that the treatment fell below accepted standards.
documents.how_to_get: Consult another qualified doctor and obtain a written opinion.

documents.name: Post-mortem report (in death cases)
documents.why: Links the cause of death to the alleged negligence.
documents.how_to_get: Obtain through the police / hospital where a post-mortem was conducted.

steps: Obtain the complete medical records in writing from the hospital.
Get an independent expert opinion on whether the standard of care was breached.
File a complaint before the Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission for compensation (medical service is covered as a 'service').
You may also complain to the State Medical Council for disciplinary action against the doctor.
In serious cases involving death or grievous harm, a criminal complaint may also be filed.

deadlines.what: Consumer complaint for deficient medical service
deadlines.timeframe: Within 2 years from the date the cause of action arose, under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (delay condonable for sufficient cause)

deadlines.what: Ordinary civil suit for damages
deadlines.timeframe: Generally within 3 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

tips: You have a right to your own medical records; ask in writing and keep the request copy.
Not every bad outcome is negligence; an expert opinion strengthens a genuine case.
Preserve original reports and imaging films.
templates.helpline: National Consumer Helpline 1915; State Medical Council

templates.title: Online Blackmail, Sextortion or Cyber Harassment
templates.title_hi: ऑनलाइन ब्लैकमेल, सेक्सटॉर्शन या साइबर उत्पीड़न
templates.description: What to do if someone is threatening to leak your photos/videos, blackmailing you online, or harassing you through the internet.

documents.name: Screenshots of threats
documents.why: Primary evidence of the blackmail or harassment.
documents.how_to_get: Capture the chats, profile, phone number/handle, and any demands for money, with visible dates and times.

documents.name: Transaction proof (if you paid)
documents.why: Helps trace the offender and recover money.
documents.how_to_get: Save UPI/bank records of any payment made to the blackmailer.

documents.name: The offending links / accounts
documents.why: Needed to request takedown and to trace the accused.
documents.how_to_get: Note the URLs, usernames, and platform on which the content or threats appear.

steps: Do not pay and do not give in to further demands; paying rarely stops blackmail.
Preserve all evidence and stop deleting chats.
Report immediately on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) and call 1930.
File a complaint at the cyber police station under the relevant IT Act and BNS provisions.
Use the platform's reporting/takedown tools to remove the content and block the offender.

deadlines.what: Reporting cyber blackmail / harassment
deadlines.timeframe: As soon as possible; there is no benefit in waiting, and quick reporting improves the chance of tracing the offender and removing content

tips: A dedicated women-and-children reporting option lets you report certain content anonymously.
Never share intimate media; once shared it can be misused.
Talking to a trusted person or counsellor helps; you are the victim, not at fault.
templates.helpline: Cyber Crime Helpline 1930; report at cybercrime.gov.in; Women Helpline 181

templates.title: Wrongful or Unfair Termination from Job
templates.title_hi: नौकरी से गलत या अनुचित बर्खास्तगी
templates.description: How to challenge a dismissal that was carried out without notice, without due process, or against labour law.

documents.name: Appointment and service records
documents.why: Establish your terms of employment and length of service.
documents.how_to_get: Appointment letter, employment contract, and salary slips.

documents.name: Termination letter
documents.why: Shows the reason and manner of dismissal.
documents.how_to_get: Keep the termination or relieving letter; if none was given, note that in writing.

documents.name: Evidence of unfairness
documents.why: Supports a claim of victimisation or lack of due process.
documents.how_to_get: Emails, performance records, and any proof that no proper inquiry or notice was given.

steps: Send a written representation to the employer asking for reasons and reinstatement or dues.
For a 'workman' under the Industrial Disputes Act, raise an industrial dispute; the Labour Commissioner attempts conciliation and may refer it to the Labour Court/Tribunal.
Managerial/other employees may need to file a civil suit or use their contract's dispute mechanism.
Claim your final settlement dues (notice pay, gratuity, unpaid salary) separately.
Seek free legal aid from the DLSA if needed.

deadlines.what: Application to the Labour Court on dismissal/discharge (Industrial Disputes Act, Section 2A)
deadlines.timeframe: Within 3 years from the date of dismissal/discharge

deadlines.what: Ordinary civil suit relating to the contract
deadlines.timeframe: Generally within 3 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

tips: Retrenchment of eligible workmen requires notice, notice pay, and compensation under the law.
Collect your evidence before you lose access to work email or systems.
Gratuity is payable after 5 years of continuous service regardless of the reason for exit (except for specified misconduct).

templates.title: Complaint Against Police Misconduct
templates.title_hi: पुलिस के दुर्व्यवहार के खिलाफ शिकायत
templates.description: How to complain about custodial abuse, illegal detention, refusal to act, corruption, or other misconduct by police officers.

documents.name: Written account of the incident
documents.why: Forms the basis of your complaint.
documents.how_to_get: Write a dated, factual account with the officer's name, rank, and station if known.

documents.name: Evidence of misconduct
documents.why: Supports the complaint.
documents.how_to_get: Medical report for any injury, photos, CCTV references, and names of witnesses.

documents.name: Related documents
documents.why: Show the context (for example an illegal detention or a demand for a bribe).
documents.how_to_get: Keep any memos, receipts, notices, or messages connected to the incident.

steps: Send a written complaint to the Superintendent of Police / senior officer above the officer complained against.
Approach the Police Complaints Authority in your state, set up following Supreme Court directions.
File a complaint with the State or National Human Rights Commission for custodial abuse or illegal detention.
You may move the jurisdictional Magistrate or the High Court for serious violations or to register an FIR against the officer.
Get any injuries medically examined immediately and preserve the report.

deadlines.what: Complaint to a Human Rights Commission
deadlines.timeframe: Generally within 1 year of the incident (NHRC ordinarily does not entertain matters older than one year)

deadlines.what: Complaint to police authorities / Magistrate
deadlines.timeframe: Act promptly; serious offences have no fixed limitation but early reporting preserves evidence

tips: Get medical documentation of any custodial injury as soon as possible.
You have a right to know the grounds of arrest and to inform a relative.
Keep copies of every complaint and its acknowledgement.
templates.helpline: State Human Rights Commission / NHRC; Police 112; NALSA 15100

templates.title: RTI First Appeal (No or Bad Reply)
templates.title_hi: आरटीआई प्रथम अपील (जवाब नहीं या गलत जवाब)
templates.description: How to file a first appeal under the Right to Information Act, 2005 when a public authority does not reply, gives an incomplete reply, or wrongly refuses information.

documents.name: Copy of the original RTI application
documents.why: Shows what information you asked for and when.
documents.how_to_get: Keep your submitted application with its date and the proof of fee payment.

documents.name: The PIO's reply (or proof of no reply)
documents.why: Establishes the ground for appeal.
documents.how_to_get: Keep the reply received; if none came, note the date the 30-day reply period ended.

documents.name: Postal / online submission proof
documents.why: Fixes the timeline for the appeal deadline.
documents.how_to_get: Registered-post receipts or the online RTI acknowledgement.

steps: Address the first appeal to the First Appellate Authority (usually an officer senior to the PIO in the same department).
State your original request, what was denied or missing, and the relief you seek.
Attach copies of the application and the reply; no fee is required for the first appeal.
If the first appeal fails, file a second appeal before the Central or State Information Commission.
Attend any hearing and keep copies of all correspondence.

deadlines.what: PIO's time to reply to an RTI application
deadlines.timeframe: 30 days from receipt (48 hours where life or liberty is involved)

deadlines.what: Filing the first appeal
deadlines.timeframe: Within 30 days of receiving the PIO's reply, or of the expiry of the 30-day reply period (the Appellate Authority may allow a later appeal for sufficient cause)

deadlines.what: Filing the second appeal to the Information Commission
deadlines.timeframe: Within 90 days of the first-appeal decision or of the date it was due

tips: The first appeal needs no fee and no lawyer.
Quote the exact points of your RTI that were not answered.
Keep the original application number handy in all correspondence.
templates.helpline: Central Information Commission / State Information Commission

templates.title: Claim Maintenance (Spouse, Child or Parent)
templates.title_hi: भरण-पोषण का दावा (पति/पत्नी, बच्चा या माता-पिता)
templates.description: How a wife, child, or parent unable to maintain themselves can claim monthly maintenance from a person legally bound to support them.

documents.name: Proof of relationship
documents.why: Establishes the legal duty to maintain you.
documents.how_to_get: Marriage certificate, birth certificate, or other proof linking you to the respondent.

documents.name: Proof of the respondent's income
documents.why: Determines the amount of maintenance.
documents.how_to_get: Salary slips, income-tax returns, property details, or any evidence of earning and lifestyle.

documents.name: Proof of your own needs
documents.why: Shows you cannot maintain yourself.
documents.how_to_get: Records of expenses, medical needs, children's school fees, and your own limited or absent income.

steps: File an application for maintenance before the Magistrate under BNSS Section 144 (earlier CrPC Section 125) - available regardless of religion.
Alternatively, use personal-law remedies (for example the Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act) or claim maintenance under the Domestic Violence Act.
Request interim maintenance so support starts while the case is pending.
Produce evidence of the respondent's income and your needs at the hearing.
Seek free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer.

deadlines.what: Application for maintenance under BNSS Section 144 (CrPC 125)
deadlines.timeframe: No fixed limitation period; it can be filed whenever the need arises, and arrears are usually recoverable within a limited past period as the court directs

tips: Senior citizens can claim maintenance quickly under the Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007.
Ask for interim maintenance early so you have support during the case.
Courts can attach salary or property if maintenance orders are ignored.
templates.helpline: Women Helpline 181; Senior Citizen Helpline 14567; NALSA 15100

templates.title: Defamation (Damage to Reputation)
templates.title_hi: मानहानि (प्रतिष्ठा को नुकसान)
templates.description: How to respond when someone spreads false statements - spoken, written, or online - that harm your reputation.

documents.name: The defamatory statement
documents.why: Central evidence of what was published and where.
documents.how_to_get: Save the article, post, message, or recording with the date, author, and platform; take clear screenshots for online content.

documents.name: Proof of falsity and harm
documents.why: Shows the statement is untrue and damaged you.
documents.how_to_get: Evidence contradicting the statement, and proof of harm such as lost work, cancelled deals, or witness accounts.

documents.name: Identity of the publisher
documents.why: Needed to serve notice and sue.
documents.how_to_get: Note the account details, website, publication, or person responsible.

steps: Preserve the defamatory content immediately before it is deleted.
Send a legal notice demanding an apology, retraction, and takedown.
For online content, use the platform's grievance/takedown process under the IT rules.
File a civil suit for damages and an injunction, and/or a criminal complaint for defamation (BNS Section 356, earlier IPC 499/500).
Keep records of all losses caused by the statement.

deadlines.what: Civil suit for damages for defamation
deadlines.timeframe: Within 1 year from the date the defamatory words were published/spoken, under the Limitation Act, 1963

deadlines.what: Criminal complaint for defamation
deadlines.timeframe: Generally within 3 years, as defamation is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years (limitation for such offences is 3 years under the CrPC/BNSS)

tips: Truth spoken for the public good is a valid defence, so make sure the statement is actually false.
Screenshot online content with the URL and date visible before it disappears.
A well-drafted legal notice often achieves a retraction without a full trial.

templates.title: Know and Enforce Your Tenant Rights
templates.title_hi: किरायेदार के अधिकार जानें और लागू करें
templates.description: A general guide for tenants on rights around rent, essential services, repairs, privacy, and lawful eviction.

documents.name: Rent agreement
documents.why: Defines the rights and duties of both sides.
documents.how_to_get: Keep your signed copy; insist on a written agreement even for informal tenancies.

documents.name: Rent payment records
documents.why: Prove you are a lawful, non-defaulting tenant.
documents.how_to_get: Bank/UPI records or written rent receipts for every payment.

documents.name: Communication with the landlord
documents.why: Evidence of repair requests, rent-hike disputes, or threats.
documents.how_to_get: Keep messages, emails, and letters, and confirm important verbal talks in writing.

steps: Insist on a written rent agreement and always get a receipt for rent paid.
Raise repair or service issues (water, electricity) with the landlord in writing.
If the landlord cuts essential services or demands an unlawful rent hike, send a written objection and, if needed, complain to the Rent Authority or police.
Remember eviction requires due legal process - a court or Rent Controller order, not force.
For disputes, approach the Rent Controller / Rent Tribunal (where the state Rent Act applies) or a civil court.

deadlines.what: Recovery of a wrongfully withheld deposit or amount
deadlines.timeframe: Within 3 years under the Limitation Act, 1963

deadlines.what: Suit for possession after forced dispossession (Specific Relief Act, Section 6)
deadlines.timeframe: Within 6 months of dispossession

deadlines.what: Rent-control notice periods and grounds
deadlines.timeframe: Governed by the specific state Rent Control or Model Tenancy Act, which varies by state

tips: A landlord cannot forcibly evict you, seize belongings, or cut utilities without a court order.
Register long-term rent agreements to make them stronger evidence.
Keep the property in good condition and pay rent on time to protect your position.
